Goodbye began as a contraction of the old blessing God be with ye.
Alphabet comes from alpha and beta, the first two letters of the Greek alphabet.
Awful originally meant full of awe, and was a compliment fit for cathedrals.
Sarcasm comes from a Greek word meaning to tear flesh like a dog.
Muscle comes from the Latin for little mouse, because a flexing bicep looked like a mouse under the skin.
Disaster literally means bad star, from the days when misfortune was blamed on the heavens.
A companion is literally someone you share bread with, from the Latin com and panis.
Window comes from the Old Norse vindauga, which means wind eye.
In nightmare, the mare was a demon believed to sit on sleepers' chests, not a horse.
Clue comes from clew, a ball of thread, like the one that guided Theseus out of the labyrinth.
Deadline once meant a literal line at Civil War prison camps; prisoners who crossed it were shot.
Quarantine comes from the Italian for forty days, the isolation period imposed on plague ships.
Robot entered English from a 1920 Czech play; robota means forced labor.
Nerd first appeared in print in a 1950 Dr. Seuss book called If I Ran the Zoo.
OK started in 1839 as a jokey Boston newspaper abbreviation of oll korrect, a comic misspelling of all correct.
Checkmate comes from the Persian phrase shah mat, meaning the king is helpless.
Barbarian began as Greek mockery, because foreign speech sounded like bar bar bar to Athenian ears.
Ostracize comes from ostrakon, the pottery shard Athenians used when voting to exile someone.
Galaxy comes from the Greek word for milk, which is why we call our own galaxy the Milky Way.
Tragedy literally means goat song in Greek, and scholars still argue about why.
Idiot originally meant a private citizen in ancient Greece, someone who took no part in public life.
Helicopter splits into helico plus pter, meaning spiral wing, not heli plus copter.
Pencil and penicillin share a root: the Latin penicillus, meaning little brush.
Money comes from Moneta, a title of the goddess Juno, because Rome's mint stood beside her temple.
Dollar descends from thaler, a silver coin first minted in a Bohemian valley called Joachimsthal.
Four is the only English number word with the same number of letters as its value.
Forty is the only number word in English whose letters appear in alphabetical order.
Boycott comes from Charles Boycott, an Irish land agent whose community froze him out in 1880.
Maverick comes from Samuel Maverick, a Texan rancher who refused to brand his cattle.
Gerrymander blends Governor Elbridge Gerry with salamander, after a twisted district map drawn in 1812.
Dunce comes from Duns Scotus, a brilliant medieval philosopher whose followers were later ridiculed.
Spoonerism honors Reverend William Spooner, famous for swapping sounds, as in a blushing crow for a crushing blow.
Bluetooth is named after Harald Bluetooth, a Viking king, and its logo merges his two runic initials.
Spam email takes its name from a Monty Python sketch in which the word spam drowns out everything else.
Jumbo came to mean huge only after Jumbo the elephant became a worldwide celebrity in the 1880s.
Grammar and glamour were once the same word; in Scotland, grammar came to mean magic.
Serendipity was coined in 1754, inspired by a Persian fairy tale about the three princes of Serendip.
Freelance was popularized by Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe, describing a mercenary whose lance was free for hire.
Loophole originally meant a narrow slit in a castle wall for shooting arrows through.
Eavesdropping originally meant lurking in the eavesdrip of a house to overhear the people inside.
Caught red handed comes from old Scottish law, where blood on the hands proved poaching or murder.
Reading someone the riot act was once literal; British officials read the law aloud to order crowds to disperse.
Slush fund comes from sailors selling slush, the greasy fat skimmed from a ship's cooking pots.
Limelight was real light; theaters once lit their stages by heating lime until it glowed brilliant white.
Rule of thumb comes from rough measuring with the thumb; the tale about a wife-beating law is a myth.
Nobody knows where the whole nine yards comes from; it is one of etymology's most famous unsolved puzzles.
Ampersand comes from and per se and, chanted by schoolchildren at the end of the alphabet.
That little dot over a lowercase i or j is called a tittle.
Ye in ye olde was never pronounced with a y sound; it is just the, written with the lost letter thorn.
Xmas is not modern laziness; X has stood for Christ, from the Greek letter chi, for many centuries.
J is one of the newest letters in English; for centuries, i and j were treated as the same letter.
Typewriter can be typed entirely on the top letter row of a standard keyboard.
Stressed spelled backward is desserts; reversible word pairs like that are called semordnilaps.
Rhythm is one of the longest common English words without an a, e, i, o, or u.
Queue keeps exactly the same sound even if you delete its last four letters.
English lets the letters o u g h be said at least six ways, as in through, though, tough, cough, bough, and thought.
A 1939 novel called Gadsby runs about fifty thousand words without using the letter e even once.
The longest word in major English dictionaries has 45 letters and names a lung disease caused by fine volcanic dust.
Fear of long words is jokingly called hippopotomonstrosesquipedaliophobia.
Buffalo can form a grammatical English sentence when repeated eight times in a row.
Go is a complete English sentence; the subject you is simply understood.
A Welsh village adopted a name of 58 letters in the 1860s, largely to lure railway tourists.
Bangkok's full ceremonial name runs well over 100 letters, and Thai schoolchildren learn it as a song.
Papua New Guinea is home to over 800 languages, more than any other country on Earth.
Roughly 7,000 languages are spoken today, and one falls silent every few weeks.
Basque is a language isolate, related to no other known living language.
Words like mama and papa sound similar worldwide because they use the first sounds babies can make.
Silbo Gomero, a whistled form of Spanish, lets islanders talk across the deep ravines of La Gomera.
Hangul, the Korean alphabet, was deliberately invented under King Sejong in the 1440s.
Sequoyah invented a writing system for Cherokee even though he could not read any existing language.
Ancient Greek was sometimes written boustrophedon, reversing direction each line like an ox plowing a field.
Our A B C letter order is more than 3,000 years old, inherited from Phoenician and even older scripts.
Louis Braille devised his reading system at age fifteen, adapting a military code for night writing.
Esperanto was invented in 1887, and today it has native speakers who grew up with it at home.
Hamlet has been translated into Klingon, a language invented for Star Trek.
Emoji is Japanese for picture character; its echo of the English word emotion is pure coincidence.
Tsundoku is a Japanese word for buying books and letting them pile up unread.
A 63 letter German word about a beef labeling law was officially retired in 2013.
Languages that got tea by sea say tea; those that got it overland say chai. Both roots are Chinese.
Coach, the vehicle and the word, traces to Kocs, a Hungarian town once famous for carriage making.
Algorithm preserves the name of al-Khwarizmi, a ninth century mathematician from Central Asia.
Magazine comes from an Arabic word for storehouse, which is why guns and periodicals share the name.
Alcohol comes from al-kuhl, an Arabic term for a fine powder used as eyeliner.
Kangaroo does not mean I don't know; it comes from a genuine Aboriginal word for the animal.
Denim means from Nimes, a city in France, while jeans are named after the Italian city of Genoa.
Bikini was named after Bikini Atoll right after nuclear tests there; its designer wanted an explosive debut.
Orange the fruit came first; before the fruit arrived, English speakers called the color yellow red.
Pink the color is named after a flower called the pink, not the other way around.
Cliche and stereotype both began as printers' terms for plates that reprinted the same text over and over.
Hello owes its dominance to the telephone; Edison pushed hello while Bell preferred ahoy.
Misheard lyrics are called mondegreens, coined in 1954 after laid him on the green was heard as Lady Mondegreen.
Dord sat in Webster's dictionary for years, defined as density; it began as a misread note card.
Esquivalience was a fake word planted in an Oxford dictionary to catch anyone copying its entries.
One prolific early contributor to the Oxford English Dictionary mailed in thousands of quotations from an asylum.
Noah Webster deliberately reshaped American spelling, dropping the u from colour and flipping theatre to theater.
Shakespeare's plays give us the first known written uses of hundreds of words, including swagger and gloomy.
Petrichor, the smell of rain on dry ground, was coined by two Australian scientists in 1964.
Defenestration means the act of throwing someone out of a window.
An eggcorn is a misheard phrase that still makes sense, named after someone writing eggcorn for acorn.
Echo was a nymph in Greek myth, cursed so that she could only repeat the words of others.
Panic comes from the god Pan, whose sudden cry was said to scatter travelers and whole armies.
Scapegoat was coined by the Bible translator William Tyndale in 1530.
Quisling became a word for traitor within days of the Norwegian collaborator's power grab in 1940.
With originally meant against in Old English, a sense that survives in withstand.
Engineers who found a moth inside a computer in 1947 taped it into the logbook; bug was already old slang for a glitch.
Sabotage comes from sabot, a wooden shoe, but the tale of workers jamming machines with their shoes is a myth.
Laconic comes from Laconia, home of the Spartans, who once answered a lengthy threat with the single word If.
Z was dropped from the early Latin alphabet and only re-added at the end, which is why it still comes last.
